---
title: "Gating as Fine-Tuning on an Orbit: Critical Commentary"
subtitle: "What the O(n) gating hypothesis actually claims, what is provable, what is only measurable, and what to do when the measurement says no"
date: 2026-06-13
abstract: |
  These notes critically examine the core hypothesis behind signed log-gate
  controllers: that element-wise multiplicative gating of layer outputs —
  $O(n)$ parameters per site — can stand in for weight updates that cost
  $O(n^2)$ (full fine-tuning) or $O(rn)$ (low-rank adaptation), with the
  NTK-mirror machinery identifying the conditions under which the two are
  equivalent. The examination yields a layered verdict. What is provable is
  an exact *orbit characterization*: gating equals weight updates of the
  row-scaling form $\Delta W = (D-I)W$, nothing more, for all inputs,
  unconditionally. What is provable *conditionally* is a four-line coherence
  theorem: out-of-orbit updates are matched in effect exactly when the
  task's adaptation signal is feature-proportional across the data. What
  can only be *measured* is whether a given task satisfies those conditions
  — and that is the true role of the NTK-mirror apparatus: a test, not a
  proof. The notes then confront the hard questions: duality residuals vary
  across batches (duality is a distribution, not a scalar, and the
  deployable controller answers a different question than the per-batch
  test); the gating idea itself is prior art (IA³, SSF, BitFit), so the
  contribution must be located in the instrument, the task-novelty
  reframing, and field-locked fitting; and when the residual is large the
  method offers diagnosis plus a constructive specification of the low-rank
  supplement — not a remedy of its own. Marketing framings ("LoRA killer",
  "fine-tuning free") are replaced with a defensible one.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
execute:
  enabled: false
---

# Scope and posture {#sec-scope}

This document is commentary, not specification. The
[method specification](scientific_method.qmd) says what the system does;
the [first-principles report](ntk_duality_first_principles.qmd) derives the
duality machinery and works two examples in closed form. These notes do
something different: they take the project's core hypothesis seriously
enough to attack it — sharpening what is actually claimed, separating
theorem from measurement, locating the genuine contribution against prior
art, and asking what happens when the measurement returns bad news. The
notes originate from a review hypothesis posed against the repository:

> Instead of updating weights via LoRA or other means — $O(n^2)$ parameters
> for $n\times n$ matrices — add a gating layer that element-wise
> multiplies layer outputs, adding only $O(n)$ parameters. The NTK-mirror
> theory justifies this by identifying the conditions under which the two
> are equivalent. If correct, a simplified proof should exist. Claims that
> the method is a "LoRA killer" or "fine-tuning free" are technically
> incorrect: the real distinction is that the implementation can separate
> adaptation from the base model via hooks.

The verdict, developed below: the hypothesis survives with two amendments
(the comparison axis is *direction of expressivity*, not parameter count;
and the NTK machinery is the *measuring device* for the equivalence
conditions, not their justification), the simplified proof exists and is
genuinely simple, and the framing corrections are warranted — with sharper
replacements available.

# What gating is in weight space: the orbit {#sec-orbit}

## The exact characterization

Place a gate immediately after a linear map $W$ (no bias in between). For
**every input simultaneously**:

$$
e^{s}\odot(Wx) \;=\; (DW)\,x,
\qquad D = \mathrm{diag}(e^{s}),
\tag{1}
$$

so gating the output of $W$ is *identically* the weight update
$\Delta W = (D-I)\,W$ — a rescaling of the rows of the existing matrix.
Call $\{DW : D \text{ positive diagonal}\}$ the **orbit** of $W$ under
gating. Three structural facts follow immediately, and each corrects a
common intuition:

**Gating is not low-rank.** $(D-I)W$ has rank up to $n$. At matched
parameter budget — rank-1 LoRA also costs $O(n)$ — the two families are
*transverse*, not nested: rank-1 LoRA ($\Delta W = ab^{\top}$) can create
one genuinely **new** output direction $a$, which no gate setting ever can;
gating produces a full-rank update confined to rescalings of existing rows,
which no rank-1 update ever can. Their intersection is the thin set of
single-row rescalings. "Fewer parameters" is therefore not the deep
comparison; **which directions of update space each family can reach** is.
Gating can amplify or attenuate features the network already computes;
it cannot rotate them or create new ones.

**Homogeneous activations preserve the orbit.** For positively
1-homogeneous $\phi$ (ReLU family), $e^{s}\odot\phi(a) =
\phi(e^{s}\odot a)$, so a gate placed after the activation is *still*
exactly a row-rescaling of the upstream matrix. For GELU/SiLU the identity
is approximate (they are near-homogeneous away from the origin) — the
orbit picture survives qualitatively but not exactly.

**Normalization eats part of the orbit.** In a real transformer block, the
gated hidden state meets an RMSNorm/LayerNorm at the next block, which
divides out per-token scale. The *uniform* component of $e^{s}$ is
annihilated; only the per-channel **anisotropy** survives, as a
redirection of the residual stream inside the normalization sphere. The
honest mental model is not "scales features" but "re-aims the residual
stream"; effective degrees of freedom are nearer $n-1$ per site. (The
duality instrument is immune to this — its tangent is measured through the
true network — but intuition built on raw scaling is not.)

## Two hook sites are two orbits

Gating a block's *output* realizes $\Delta W = (D-I)W$ — row scaling of
the matrix upstream. Gating a block's *input* realizes
$\Delta W' = W'(D-I)$ — **column** scaling of the matrix downstream. These
are different families; their union is strictly larger than either, though
still diagonal-structured (no new directions). The hook-site ablation that
the method prescribes for theorem-facing claims is therefore not hygiene
but substance: it changes which orbit is being tested.

# The shared-gate constraint and the simplified proof {#sec-proof}

## Where the real bottleneck is

Parameter count is not the binding constraint; **sharedness** is. A weight
update $\delta W$ produces an *input-dependent additive* change
$\delta W\,x$; a gate produces an *activation-proportional* change
$s\odot h(x)$ with the **same** $s$ for every token and example. On one
example the two can be matched exactly; across a distribution they
diverge whenever different inputs demand different per-channel scalings.
This observation yields the simplified proof the hypothesis asked for, as
a three-rung ladder.

## Rung 1 — the orbit identity (exact, global)

Equation (1). Gating $\equiv$ weight updates of the form $(D-I)W$, for all
inputs, with no conditions. One line. Equally a theorem in the other
direction: any update *not* of this form is not exactly matched by any
gate setting, as a function.

## Rung 2 — the coherence theorem (exact, per-distribution)

Let $\delta h_{jt}$ be the activation perturbation a desired update would
induce at channel $j$, token $t$, and $h_{jt}$ the current activation. The
best linearized gate solves a channel-separable least squares:

$$
\min_{s_j}\ \sum_t \big(\delta h_{jt} - s_j\, h_{jt}\big)^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
s_j^{\star} = \frac{\sum_t h_{jt}\,\delta h_{jt}}{\sum_t h_{jt}^2},
\qquad
\text{residual}_j = \big(1-\rho_j^2\big)\sum_t \delta h_{jt}^2,
\tag{2}
$$

where $\rho_j$ is the (uncentered) correlation between $h_{j\cdot}$ and
$\delta h_{j\cdot}$ across the token distribution. **Gates realize exactly
the component of the desired change that is proportional to what each
channel already computes; the unrealizable energy per channel is
$(1-\rho_j^2)$.** Four lines, no kernels, no solvers. Call $\rho_j$ the
channel's **coherence** for this task.

Two corollaries tie the method's pieces into one picture:

- **Gate training *is* coherence regression.** The gate gradient at
  $s=0$ is $\partial L/\partial s_j = \sum_t g_{jt}h_{jt}$ — the numerator
  of $s_j^{\star}$ (with $\delta h = -g$ the steepest-descent direction in
  activation space). Preconditioning by the activation metric
  $M_j = \sum_t h_{jt}^2$ — i.e. natural gradient in the method's own
  pullback metric — yields exactly $s_j^{\star}$. Ordinary gate fitting,
  in the right metric, is channel-wise regression onto the realizable
  component; nothing else was ever going to be learned.
- **The saliency rule has a testable flaw.** Gate selection scores
  channels by $\sum_t |g_{jt}h_{jt}|$. A *sign-incoherent* channel — large
  per-token products with a cancelling sum, $\rho_j\approx 0$ — passes
  this score while being precisely **unrealizable** by a shared gate. The
  coherent score $|\sum_t g_{jt}h_{jt}|$, or directly
  $\rho_j^2\times$energy, should select strictly more useful gates. This
  is a falsifiable improvement the coherence view predicts.

## Rung 3 — the NTK specialization

Set $\delta h$ to the activation field induced by an actual SGD step and
compose with the downstream Jacobian onto an observable. The
[first-principles report](ntk_duality_first_principles.qmd) carries this
out: the contribution of the weight matrix immediately *upstream* of a
gate site is absorbed exactly, channel by channel (visibility and
controllability coincide under ReLU-type activations); contributions of
*downstream* matrices reduce to a span condition over what the gated
channels feed into. Collecting the premises, equivalence of effect holds
under:

- **C1 (coherence):** the task's gradient field is feature-proportional at
  the gated sites — $\rho_j \to 1$ on selected channels;
- **C2 (readout coverage):** field components attributable to layers
  downstream of all gates lie in the span of what the gated sites feed
  into (gating every block puts every weight matrix upstream of some gate,
  which is the structural fix);
- **C3 (smallness):** the box and chart curvature keep the realized step
  near the tangent;
- **C4 (persistence):** C1–C3 continue to hold along the adaptation path,
  with accumulated error bounded by the per-step residual ledger.

# Theorem versus test: three tiers {#sec-tiers}

The phrase "NTK-mirror justifies the equivalence" conflates three
epistemically different layers. Keeping them separate is the single most
important clarification of these notes.

| Tier | Status | Content |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Theorem, unconditional** | the orbit identity (1): which weight updates gating matches exactly, for all inputs — and which it provably never does |
| 2 | **Theorem, conditional** | the coherence theorem (2) and span condition: out-of-orbit updates are matched *in effect* iff C1–C2 hold |
| 3 | **Measurement** | whether a given task, model, dataset, and observable actually satisfy C1–C4 — decided empirically, batch by batch |

NTK-mirror — the measured target field, the adjoint-consistent solver, the
residual ladder — lives entirely in **Tier 3**. It is the instrument that
evaluates the Tier-2 premises on real networks where closed forms are
impossible. It is *not* a proof of equivalence, and cannot be: generic
weight updates are not in the orbit (dimension counting alone guarantees
it), so the unconditional equivalence claim is simply false, and any
honest theory is conditional. The machinery's virtue is exactly that it
can **refute** — a small residual says the premises held *here*, for this
batch, this observable, this state; a large one says this step wanted
something no gate setting can deliver. Mathematics tells you *what kind*
of update is matchable; the instrument tells you *whether the update your
task actually wants is of that kind*.

The reframing this licenses is worth stating: the residual does not grade
the adapter so much as it grades the **task**. It measures how much
genuinely new structure a task demands from a frozen model — adapter
adequacy and task novelty are the same measurement read from opposite
ends.

# Duality is a distribution, not a scalar {#sec-distribution}

Residuals vary across support and calibration batches. Once they do, "is
this task gate-amenable?" has no single answer, and — more subtly — the
per-batch test stops being the decision-relevant quantity.

## The per-batch test answers the wrong question for a deployed controller

The per-batch solve asks "*could some gate update imitate this step?*"
The deployed object is **one** gate vector serving the whole distribution.
The two disagree in both directions:

- **Per-batch residuals can be pessimistic.** A small batch yields a
  noisy, nearly rank-one field that points partly out of the orbit by
  chance even when the task's *mean* field is perfectly coherent. Full
  fine-tuning has the same phenomenon (per-batch gradient conflict); SGD
  averages it away, and so does gate training. The decision-relevant
  target for a static controller is the **expected** field.
- **Per-batch residuals can be optimistic.** Every batch can pass
  individually while each demands a *different* realizing update $u_b$.
  No single $s$ then serves all of them: per channel, batch A wants
  amplification where batch B wants attenuation, the shared gate fits the
  average, and the average can be near zero — destructive interference,
  worse than partial gains.

## Three diagnostics, and the regime map

| Measurement | Question answered |
|---|---|
| residual of the **mean field** (large or averaged support batches) | can one controller imitate the task's expected update? |
| **residual vs. support batch size** | does per-batch failure shrink with batch size (noise) or plateau (structure)? |
| **dispersion of per-batch realizing updates** $u_b$ (pairwise cosines) | do batches agree on *which* gate motion they want? |

(Practical hygiene first: energy-weight everything by $\lVert d\rVert$ —
a large relative residual on a near-zero field is the finite-difference
noise floor, not evidence — and clear the boring causes: unconverged CG,
clipping, an unrepresentative projection.)

- **Regime 1 — mean-field residual small; per-batch residuals large but
  $u_b$ consistent.** Gradient noise. The task is gate-amenable; nothing
  is wrong; train normally.
- **Regime 2 — $u_b$ cluster; failures concentrate on identifiable input
  types.** The task is a *mixture* of sub-skills: within-cluster coherence
  is high, pooled coherence is low. The correct response is architectural
  and already exists in the system: **per-cluster controllers plus
  retrieval** — the persistent-memory subsystem. This analysis gives that
  subsystem a first-principles justification it did not previously have:
  controller memory is not a storage convenience but the *correct response
  to batch-dependent duality residuals*. Per-example residuals are
  themselves a usable routing signal.
- **Regime 3 — residual large even on the mean field; $u_b$ near
  isotropic.** The task genuinely demands out-of-orbit structure: new
  feature directions, sign flips (forbidden by $e^{s}>0$), suppression
  beyond the box, token-position-specific edits (forbidden by
  sharedness). No gate budget fixes this — more gates fix *coverage*
  failures, never *coherence* failures (check which: re-run with all
  channels gated; a surviving residual is coherence). Remedies in
  @sec-remedies.

The $u_b$-dispersion diagnostic does not exist in the repository today; it
would be a cheap and genuinely informative addition to the dual
diagnostics.

# What, then, is the contribution? {#sec-contribution}

## What is not new

Element-wise multiplicative activation scaling is established PEFT prior
art: IA³ (Liu et al., 2022) rescales keys, values, and FFN activations
with learned vectors; SSF (Lian et al., 2022) learns per-channel scale
*and shift*; BitFit (Ben Zaken et al., 2022) is the additive $O(n)$
analogue; activation steering vectors are the additive activation-control
cousin. If the claimed contribution were "adapt with $O(n)$ multiplicative
parameters," it would be a rediscovery. Equally non-differentiating:
log-space composition (weight deltas also add exactly in their own linear
coordinates) and hook detachability (modern serving stacks hot-swap LoRA
adapters too).

## What is new, and worth defending

1. **A family-agnostic adequacy instrument.** Nothing in the pipeline —
   measure the true SGD field by a reversible step, build an adapter
   family's tangent, project, read a residual ladder with validity probes
   — is specific to gates. $B$ could be the tangent of low-rank updates,
   biases, prefixes, or any parameterized intervention. What was built is
   a **pre-training, measured answer to "which PEFT family suffices for
   this task on this model?"** — a question currently settled by folklore
   and sweeps. Instruments of this kind are real contributions; the
   thermometer is not a theory of heat, but there is no theory of heat
   without it.
2. **The task-novelty reframing.** The residual quantifies, per example,
   how much a task asks a frozen model for structure it does not already
   have. That is a measurement with uses beyond adapter choice: routing,
   curriculum, dataset characterization.
3. **Field-locked fitting as a training principle.** Fitting an adapter by
   *imitating the measured optimization field* rather than descending the
   loss is a distinct procedure — distillation of training dynamics — and
   is what makes per-step duality claims constructive.

## Corrected framing

"LoRA killer" and "fine-tuning free" should be retired. The method **is**
fine-tuning: the gates are parameters trained by backpropagation through
the frozen model ("forward-pass" describes serving, not fitting).
Hook-separability is an implementation property, as the review hypothesis
correctly noted, and is not unique. The defensible statement:

> **Weight-preserving, orbit-restricted, detachable fine-tuning, with a
> measurable adequacy test.** The update is confined to a bounded,
> interpretable family (per-channel amplification within
> $e^{\pm s_{\max}}$); whether that family suffices for a task is measured
> before relying on it, not assumed.

One alternative explanation must also stay on the table until tested:
NLL gains from gate fitting may partly be a *calibration* effect
(near-uniform late-layer scaling acts like a temperature knob), not task
learning. The repository's own prescribed controls — random gates,
layer-restricted gates, exact-answer metrics alongside NLL — are exactly
the discriminating experiments; they should be reported with any claim.

# When the task is out-of-orbit: remedies and non-remedies {#sec-remedies}

Suppose the measurement comes back bad: large mean-field residual, the
task demands low-rank, out-of-orbit structure. What does NTK-mirror offer?

**Strictly: diagnosis, not cure.** Every deployable knob — more gates,
bigger box, either hook site — enlarges coverage *of the orbit*, never
escapes it. This should be said plainly. But three qualifications make
the answer more useful than "no":

1. **A remedy of degree: the hook-site union.** Output-gating and
   input-gating are different orbits (row- vs column-scaling,
   @sec-orbit); their union is measurably larger, and the instrument can
   price exactly how much it buys per task.
2. **An out-of-orbit surface already inside the system.** The
   hallucination verifier's KV order-debias solves a per-input ridge
   projection for a **dense additive delta on the value cache**. Additive
   cache deltas escape both fundamental constraints: they act on silent
   channels (not activation-proportional) and they are solved *per input*
   (no sharedness). The system thus already contains an out-of-orbit,
   per-request control surface — used today only inside the verifier.
   Promoting per-request closed-form activation steering to a first-class
   adaptation mode is the most natural internal extension; its costs
   (per-request compute, no persistent artifact) are the honest trade.
3. **The residual is constructive: it specifies the supplement.** The
   unrealized component $d_\perp = d - Bu$ is a measured vector, not a
   score. Pulled back to weight space per layer, its singular-value
   spectrum **is** the minimal-rank requirement of the out-of-orbit part
   of the task. The principled hybrid writes itself: *gates absorb the
   orbit component at $O(n)$; a low-rank adapter is fitted to the residual
   field with rank read off its spectrum rather than guessed; the same
   instrument then re-certifies the combined tangent
   $[\,B_{\mathrm{gate}}\,|\,B_{\mathrm{lora}}\,]$.* In this design LoRA
   is not a competitor to be killed but the component the instrument
   **prescribes and sizes** when gates are not enough. A gentler in-family
   step exists too: per-channel additive shifts alongside scales
   (SSF-style) break activation-proportionality without leaving $O(n)$ —
   and the repository's diffusion experiment already trains shifts; the
   language-model path simply never adopted them.

The mature claim, replacing the marketing one: **spend the adaptation
budget where the measured field says the structure is — and here is the
device that measures it.**

# Falsifiable predictions and proposed instruments {#sec-predictions}

Concrete consequences of this analysis, each testable with the existing
or lightly extended toolkit:

1. **Coherence profile (new, cheap).** One backward pass recording $h$ and
   $\partial L/\partial h$ per site; report the $\rho_j^2$-weighted
   realizable energy fraction per layer. This is the duality residual
   specialized to the activation observable — no JVP, no CG — and should
   *predict before fitting* whether a task is gate-amenable. Prediction:
   tasks with a high coherence fraction at salient sites show NLL gains
   from gate fitting; low-coherence tasks do not.
2. **Coherent saliency (modification).** Select gates by
   $|\sum_t g_{jt}h_{jt}|$ (or $\rho_j^2\times$energy) instead of
   $\sum_t|g_{jt}h_{jt}|$. Prediction: matched-budget controllers improve,
   most on tasks with many sign-incoherent channels.
3. **$u_b$-dispersion diagnostic (new).** Report pairwise cosines of
   per-batch realizing updates alongside the residuals. Prediction: it
   separates mixture tasks (Regime 2 — fix with memory routing) from
   noise (Regime 1) and from genuine out-of-orbit demand (Regime 3) where
   the residual alone cannot.
4. **Residual-vs-batch-size curve (protocol).** Prediction: noise-driven
   residuals decay with support batch size; structural ones plateau.
5. **Task-class predictions.** Gate-amenable: style, register, format,
   calibration, domain emphasis, procedure priming — distribution-level
   reweighting of existing features. Gate-resistant: new factual bindings,
   new token mappings, counterfactual re-binding, edits requiring sign
   flips or >$\,e^{s_{\max}}{-}1$ suppression, token-position-specific
   corrections. Note that the resistant list independently re-derives the
   project's documented memory-scope boundary ("behavior, not facts") —
   theory and operating guidance agree, which is evidence the theory is
   tracking the right mechanism.
6. **Calibration control.** Compare full controllers against
   late-layer-only and uniform-scale controllers on exact-answer metrics.
   Prediction: if gains are calibration, the restricted variants match on
   NLL but not on exact answers.

# Summary verdict {#sec-verdict}

- The $O(n)$-vs-$O(n^2)$ framing is correct arithmetic but the wrong
  axis: gating is not a smaller version of weight updating, it is a
  **different, transverse family** — the row-scaling orbit — that can
  only amplify or attenuate existing features.
- The simplified proof exists: orbit identity → coherence theorem →
  NTK specialization, with conditions C1–C4. The activation metric turns
  out to be the choice that makes gate training literally equal
  channel-wise regression onto the realizable component.
- **NTK-mirror is the test, not the proof.** Tier 1 and 2 are theorems;
  whether a task satisfies their premises is Tier 3 — an empirical,
  batch-indexed measurement that can refute.
- Duality is a **distribution** over data, and the deployable controller
  answers a different question than the per-batch test; mean-field
  residuals, batch-size scaling, and update dispersion are the
  decision-relevant statistics. Mixture-type failures are the
  first-principles justification for the controller-memory subsystem.
- The gating mechanism is prior art; the durable contributions are the
  **family-agnostic adequacy instrument**, the **task-novelty
  reframing**, and **field-locked fitting**.
- For out-of-orbit tasks the method offers diagnosis, a per-input escape
  hatch it has not yet generalized (additive cache deltas), and — most
  valuably — a **constructive sizing of the low-rank supplement** from the
  residual spectrum.
- Replace "LoRA-free / fine-tuning free / LoRA killer" with:
  *weight-preserving, orbit-restricted, detachable fine-tuning with a
  measurable adequacy test.*

# References {#sec-refs .unnumbered}

- Liu, H., Tam, D., Muqeeth, M., Mohta, J., Huang, T., Bansal, M.,
  Raffel, C. (2022). *Few-Shot Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning is Better
  and Cheaper than In-Context Learning* (IA³). NeurIPS.
- Lian, D., Zhou, D., Feng, J., Wang, X. (2022). *Scaling & Shifting Your
  Features: A New Baseline for Efficient Model Tuning* (SSF). NeurIPS.
- Ben Zaken, E., Ravfogel, S., Goldberg, Y. (2022). *BitFit: Simple
  Parameter-efficient Fine-tuning for Transformer-based Masked
  Language-models.* ACL.
- Hu, E., Shen, Y., Wallis, P., Allen-Zhu, Z., Li, Y., Wang, S., Wang,
  L., Chen, W. (2022). *LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language
  Models.* ICLR.
- Jacot, A., Gabriel, F., Hongler, C. (2018). *Neural Tangent Kernel:
  Convergence and Generalization in Neural Networks.* NeurIPS.
- Chizat, L., Oyallon, E., Bach, F. (2019). *On Lazy Training in
  Differentiable Programming.* NeurIPS.
